moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the Queen's Gambit Accepted, where Black captures the c4 pawn rather than declining with ...e6. White plays 1.d4, claiming central space. The QGA leads to open, dynamic positions where White gets a strong center but Black gets active piece play and a healthy pawn structure.
plans: Play c4 next to challenge Black's center
Build a broad pawn center
alternatives: e4 -- leads to more open, tactical games
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: In the Queen's Gambit Accepted, Black captures c4 for active piece play and a healthy pawn structure, while White claims the center with d4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds with 1...d5, matching White's central presence. The d5 pawn controls c4 and e4, establishing Black's stake in the center before the gambit arrives.
plans: Hold the center, develop pieces naturally
alternatives: Nf6 -- the Indian approach, more flexible
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1...d5 matches White's central presence, controlling c4 and e4 before the gambit.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays 2.c4, the Queen's Gambit. The c4 pawn challenges d5, offering Black a critical choice: decline (with ...e6 or ...c6) for solidity, or accept (with ...dxc4) for active piece play.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Central tension: White d4+c4 vs Black d5
plans: If ...dxc4, recapture the pawn or play e4 with a huge center
If ...e6, maintain pressure on d5
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's c4 pawn challenges d5, offering Black a choice: decline with ...e6 or ...c6 for solidity, or accept ...dxc4 for active piece play.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black accepts with 2...dxc4, the Queen's Gambit Accepted. Black gives up the center temporarily but gains the c4 pawn and freedom to develop pieces without the cramped structures of the QGD. White will regain the pawn easily — the real question is whether Black can equalize before White's central advantage becomes decisive.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black surrenders the center temporarily; White has a d4 pawn vs nothing
plans: Develop quickly, play ...c5 to challenge d4
Don't try to hold the c4 pawn -- focus on activity
alternatives: e6 -- the QGD, solid but the bishop gets locked in
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black accepts the Queen's Gambit, giving up the center for the c4 pawn and free development, but White regains it easily — the question is whether Black equalizes before White's central edge becomes decisive.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to f3, prioritizing development over immediately recapturing on c4. The Nf3 supports d4, controls e5, and prepares kingside castling. White knows the c4 pawn will be recovered — rushing to take it back would waste development time.
plans: Develop with e3 + Bxc4 to recover the pawn naturally
Keep flexible development
alternatives: e3 -- also good, immediately opening the bishop's diagonal
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White develops Nf3, supporting d4 and kingside castling, delaying the c4-pawn recapture to avoid wasting tempo.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, controlling d5 and e4 — the two squares White wants for pawns. The Nf6 prevents an easy e4, develops a piece, and prepares kingside castling. This is the most active defensive response.
plans: Control e4, prepare ...e6 and ...c5
alternatives: a6 -- preparing ...b5 to hold the pawn, but this is considered slightly dubio...
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight on f6 controls d5 and e4, blocking White's pawn advance and preparing kingside castling.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays e3, opening the f1-a6 diagonal for the bishop to recapture on c4. The e3 pawn also supports d4 and creates a solid central structure. This is the most common and practical approach to recovering the gambit pawn.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's d4-e3 structure is solid; bishop will recapture on c4
plans: Bxc4 next, then O-O with easy development
alternatives: e4 -- the central advance, more ambitious but also more committal
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's e3 opens the f1-a6 diagonal for the bishop to recapture on c4, supporting d4 and a solid center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays e6, supporting the d5 square prophylactically and opening the bishop's diagonal. The e6 pawn creates a solid foundation — even though d5 is empty, e6 prevents any d5 advances from White and prepares natural development.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's e6 pawn supports a future ...c5 break
plans: Play ...c5 to challenge d4
Develop the dark-squared bishop and castle
alternatives: Bg4 -- pinning the knight, active but the bishop can be chased with h3
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's e6 supports d5, blocks White's d5 advance, and opens the bishop's diagonal.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White recovers the pawn with Bxc4, placing the bishop on a fantastic diagonal. The Bc4 targets f7 — the weakest square near Black's castled king — along the a2-g8 diagonal. This is the payoff for patient development: White gets the pawn back with the bishop on an ideal attacking square.
plans: Castle next, complete development with a great position
The Bc4 pressures f7 and supports central play
alternatives: No real alternative -- this is the whole point of e3
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White recovers the pawn with Bxc4, targeting f7 along the a2–g8 diagonal.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes with c5, the critical central break that defines the QGA. By challenging d4 immediately, Black opens lines for piece play and prevents White from consolidating a static central advantage. If White takes cxd4, the position opens favorably for Black's active pieces.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Central tension: Black's c5 challenges White's d4 -- the position is about to open up
plans: Challenge d4, get active piece play in the center
Develop quickly and castle
alternatives: a6 -- preparing ...b5, but ...c5 is more direct and principled
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's c5 challenges d4, opening lines for piece play and preventing White from consolidating a static center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles kingside, getting the king to safety before the center opens. With c5 creating central tension, it's crucial to castle before the position becomes tactical. The Rf1 also supports a potential f4 advance.
plans: Develop queenside pieces, fight for the d-file
Consider dxc5 or maintain the tension
alternatives: dxc5 -- releasing tension immediately, but castling first is more flexible
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White castles kingside to safety, Rf1 supports f4, and c5 creates central tension.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays a6, a prophylactic move preventing Nb5 (which would target c7 and d6). The a6 pawn also prepares the ...b5 queenside expansion that is Black's main strategic plan in the QGA — gaining space and developing the Bc8 to b7.
plans: Play ...b5 to attack Bc4 and expand on the queenside
Develop ...Bb7 with a strong diagonal
alternatives: Nc6 -- developing a piece, but a6 prepares the whole queenside plan
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: a6 prevents Nb5 and prepares b5 to expand the queenside and develop the Bc8 to b7.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White places the queen on e2, connecting the rooks and supporting a future e4 central advance. The Qe2 is flexible — it supports e3-e4, keeps an eye on the a6-f1 diagonal, and prepares Rd1 to increase central pressure.
plans: Prepare e4 central advance
Support the bishop on c4 against ...b5
alternatives: a4 -- preventing ...b5 directly, but Qe2 is more ambitious
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Qe2 connects rooks, supports e3-e4, eyes the a6-f1 diagonal, and prepares Rd1 for central pressure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black pushes b5, executing the queenside expansion. The b5 pawn attacks the Bc4, forces it to retreat, and claims space on the queenside. This is the heart of Black's QGA strategy — gain queenside space, develop the Bc8 to b7, and create counterplay on the long diagonal.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black has a queenside pawn majority that can create a passed pawn later
plans: Develop Bb7 on the long diagonal
Use the queenside majority as a long-term asset
alternatives: Nc6 -- developing, but ...b5 is the thematic move that defines Black's counte...
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's b5 attacks the Bc4, forcing retreat and gaining queenside space for the Bb7 long-diagonal counterplay.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The bishop retreats to b3, maintaining the powerful a2-g8 diagonal pressure. On b3, the bishop is safe from further pawn attacks, still targets f7 and e6, and supports a potential d5 or e4 central advance.
plans: Maintain diagonal pressure on e6-f7
Prepare Nc3 and e4
alternatives: Bd3 -- points at the kingside but blocks the d-file and doesn't pressure f7
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop retreats to b3, keeping pressure on f7 and e6 while supporting a central push.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black fianchettoes the bishop to b7, the strategic payoff of the ...a6-b5 plan. The Bb7 is a powerful piece — it pressures the e4 square (preventing White's ideal center), controls the long a8-h1 diagonal, and contests White's Bg2 in Catalan-type structures.
plans: Pressure the long diagonal, especially e4 and d5
Support central play from the flank
alternatives: Nbd7 -- developing, but the bishop on b7 is the whole reason Black played ......
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Bb7 pressures e4, controls the long diagonal, and contests White's Bg2.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White places the rook on d1, seizing the most important file in the position. If the center opens with dxc5 or cxd4, the d-file becomes the battleground. The Rd1 pressures d7 and prepares to control the file with doubled rooks.
plans: Control the d-file
Prepare Nc3 and e4 break
alternatives: Nc3 -- developing the knight, also very natural
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's rook on d1 seizes the critical d-file, pressuring d7 and preparing doubled rooks if the center opens.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to d7, supporting both the ...c5 and ...e5 central breaks. The Nd7 keeps the c-file clear for Black's rook and can reroute to b6 (pressuring c4/d5) or f8-g6 for kingside operations.
plans: Reroute Nd7 to b6 or e5 depending on the position
Castle and complete development
alternatives: Nc6 -- more active but blocks the c-pawn and can be hit by d5
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Nd7 supports c5/e5 breaks, keeps the c-file open, and can reroute to b6 or g6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops Nc3, the last minor piece, adding another attacker to the e4 square. The Nc3 prepares the thematic e3-e4 advance that would give White a powerful central pawn duo. With all pieces developed, White is ready for the central break.
plans: Play e4 for a dominant center
The knight also eyes d5 and b5
alternatives: e4 -- playing it immediately is possible but Nc3 first adds more support
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nc3 develops the last minor piece, adding an attacker to e4 and preparing the central e3-e4 break.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays Qb6, adding pressure to the d4 pawn and the Bb3. The Qb6 is a multipurpose move — it attacks d4, pressures b3, supports the ...c4 advance (trapping the bishop), and keeps the queen active on the queenside where Black's strengths lie.
plans: Pressure d4 and Bb3
Support queenside play with ...c4 or ...Rc8
alternatives: Be7 -- simple development, but Qb6 is more active and thematic
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Qb6 attacks d4, pressures b3, supports c4 to trap the bishop, and keeps the queen active on the queenside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, immediately staking a claim in the center and controlling the important e5 and c5 squares.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White establishes a central pawn on d4, creating the potential for a classical pawn center with c4.
plans: Prepare c4 to challenge Black's center
Develop pieces toward the center with Nf3 and Bf4 or Bg5
alternatives: 1.e4 leads to more tactical, open games
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's d4 pawn stakes the center and controls e5 and c5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds symmetrically, placing a pawn in the center and fighting for equal space. this stabilizing move controls key central squares and prepares natural piece development.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The position now features symmetrical central pawns, with both sides controlling key central squar.
plans: Prepare to deal with White's coming c4 advance
Develop pieces naturally with Nf6 and Bf5 or Bg4
alternatives: 1...Nf6 transposes to various Indian defenses
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black mirrors with e5, fighting for central space and preparing natural development.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Queen's Gambit! White offers the c4 pawn to deflect Black's d5 pawn and gain central control.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates the Queen's Gambit pawn structure, offering to trade the c4 pawn for Black's central.
plans: Regain the pawn with natural development
Build pressure on the center with pieces
alternatives: 2.Nf3 leads to quieter development without immediate central tension
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White offers the c4 pawn to deflect Black's d5 pawn and gain central control.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black accepts the Queen's Gambit, capturing the c4 pawn and temporarily gaining material.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black has captured on c4, removing the central tension but giving White a free hand in the center.
plans: Hold onto the extra pawn with ...b5
Develop quickly to avoid being overwhelmed in the center
alternatives: 2...e6 declines the gambit, leading to slower but more solid positions
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black accepts the Queen's Gambit, capturing the c4 pawn for a temporary material gain.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the king's knight to its most active square, controlling the important e5 and g5 squares while preparing to support the center.
plans: Support e4 advance to dominate the center
Develop the kingside pieces toward castling
alternatives: 3.e3 immediately prepares to recapture on c4 but is less forcing
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's knight develops to f3, controlling e5 and g5 while supporting the center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the king's knight to its ideal square, controlling the central e4 and d5 squares while preparing to castle kingside.
plans: Support ...e6 to develop the light-squared bishop
Prepare castling kingside for king safety
alternatives: 3...e6 immediately supports the center but blocks the light-squared bishop
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight develops to f3, controlling e4 and d5 and preparing kingside castling.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White prepares to recapture the c4 pawn with the light-squared bishop, maintaining central control while developing pieces harmoniously.
plans: Develop Bxc4 to regain the pawn
Complete kingside development and castle
alternatives: 4.e4 immediately grabs the center but allows Black active counterplay
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White recaptures c4 with the light-squared bishop, keeping central control and development.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strengthens the center and prepares to develop the light-squared bishop, typically to b7 via the long diagonal.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates a solid pawn structure with pawns on e6 and f7, supporting central control while pre.
plans: Develop the light-squared bishop to b7
Prepare ...c5 to challenge White's center
alternatives: 4...Bg4 pins the knight but allows White strong central play
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's 1...c5 strengthens the center and prepares the light-squared bishop to b7.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White regains the gambit pawn and develops the light-squared bishop to its most active square.
plans: Castle kingside to complete development
Apply pressure on f7 and the long diagonal
alternatives: 5.Be2 is safer but less active
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White regains the gambit pawn and develops the light-squared bishop to its most active square.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at White's central pawn, creating immediate counterplay and fighting for central equality.
plans: Develop pieces actively with tempo
Create counterplay against White's center
alternatives: 5...Be7 is quieter but allows White to consolidate
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black strikes at e4, creating counterplay and fighting for central equality.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles kingside, bringing the king to safety while connecting the rooks. this move also prepares to bring the rook to d1, putting pressure on Black's central pawns.
plans: Bring the rook to d1 to pressure the d-file
Coordinate pieces for central pressure
alternatives: 6.dxc5 releases central tension but gives Black active pieces
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White castles kingside, connects rooks, and prepares Rd1 to pressure Black's center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black prepares the thematic queenside expansion with ...b5, controlling the b5 square and preventing White pieces from landing there.
plans: Advance ...b5 to gain queenside space
Develop the bishop to b7 via the long diagonal
alternatives: 6...Nbd7 develops immediately but doesn't prepare queenside expansion
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...b5 controls b5, blocking White's pieces from landing there and preparing queenside expansion.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the queen to a central square, preparing to support the center with Rd1 and maintaining flexibility.
plans: Play Rd1 to dominate the d-file
Maintain central pressure while keeping tactical options
alternatives: 7.Re1 prepares e4 but is less flexible
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's queen centralizes to support the center with Rd1, keeping flexibility.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black executes the thematic queenside expansion, attacking White's bishop and gaining valuable space. This move forces the bishop to retreat while establishing Black's presence on the queenside.
plans: Develop Bb7 to complete the fianchetto
Use queenside space advantage for piece activity
alternatives: 7...Be7 develops but allows White to consolidate the center
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's queenside expansion forces the bishop back and gains space.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White retreats the bishop to the b3 square, maintaining its activity on the long diagonal while avoiding the pawn attack.
plans: Support central play from the long diagonal
Coordinate with other pieces for kingside pressure
alternatives: 8.Bd3 eyes h7 but is less active on the long diagonal
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White retreats the bishop to b3, keeping it active on the long diagonal and dodging the pawn attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the fianchetto development, placing the bishop on the long diagonal where it exerts maximum pressure.
plans: Use the long diagonal to create kingside threats
Support central squares with the powerful bishop
alternatives: 8...Be7 develops but is much more passive
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black fianchettoes the bishop onto the long diagonal for maximum pressure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White brings the rook to the central d-file, putting immediate pressure on Black's position and supporting the d4 pawn.
plans: Maintain pressure on the d-file
Support the d4 pawn and central control
alternatives: 9.a4 challenges Black's queenside immediately
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's rook on d1 pressures the center and supports the d4 pawn.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the queen's knight to its ideal square, supporting the center and preparing to contest White's pressure.
plans: Bring the knight to more active squares like b6 or c5
Complete development and challenge White's center
alternatives: 9...Nc6 is more aggressive but blocks the c-pawn
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's queen's knight develops to c6, supporting the center and contesting White's pressure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the queen's knight to its most active square, supporting the d4 pawn and increasing control over central squares.
plans: Support d4 and maintain central control
Coordinate pieces for potential tactics
alternatives: 10.a4 immediately challenges Black's queenside structure
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's queen's knight develops to c3, supporting d4 and controlling central squares.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black activates the queen with tempo, attacking the b2 pawn and creating immediate tactical threats.
plans: Create tactical threats against White's pawns
Coordinate queen and bishop for maximum pressure
alternatives: 10...Be7 completes development but is less active
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's queen attacks b2 with tempo, creating immediate tactical threats.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Smyslov Variation (3...a6) is an important variation of the Queen's Gambit Accepted. Smyslov's idea of 3...a6 immediately prepares ...b5 to hold the c4 pawn or at least gain queenside space.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 3...a6 prepares ...b5 to hold the c4 pawn or gain queenside space.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds with d5, contesting the center. This is the move that defines the Smyslov Variation (3...a6).
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's d5 contests the center, defining the Smyslov Variation after 3...a6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: dxc4 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: dxc4 captures the pawn, changing the pawn structure and material balance.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Rd1 places the rook on a central file where it will be most active. Rooks belong on open or semi-open files.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Rd1 places the rook on the central d-file, active on a semi-open file.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Bb3 improves the bishop's placement. The bishop was less effective on c4 and moves to b3 where it serves the plan better.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bb3 improves the bishop's placement, aiming at Black's kingside along the a2–g8 diagonal.

moveAnnotations.annotation: cxd4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: cxd4 stakes a central pawn claim, controlling space and restricting Black's piece activity.

moveAnnotations.annotation: exd4 captures the pawn. This exchange is thematic in the Smyslov Variation (3...a6) — it defines the resulting pawn structure and piece activity.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: exd4 captures the pawn, defining the Smyslov pawn structure and piece activity.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Be7. This is the typical position arising from the Smyslov Variation (3...a6). From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Be7 develops the bishop, typical of the Smyslov Variation, with plans around piece placement and pawn breaks.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Sadler Variation (3...Nf6 4.e3 Bg4) is an important variation of the Queen's Gambit Accepted.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: In the Queen's Gambit Accepted Sadler Variation, 3...Nf6 4.e3 Bg4 pins the knight.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds with d5, contesting the center. This is the move that defines the Sadler Variation (3...Nf6 4.e3 Bg4).
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's d5 contests the center, leading to the Sadler Variation with 3...Nf6 4.e3 Bg4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: dxc4 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: dxc4 captures the pawn, changing the pawn structure and material balance.

moveAnnotations.annotation: h3 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: h3 gains kingside space to support a future attack on the enemy king.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Bh5 improves the bishop's placement. The bishop was less effective on g4 and moves to h5 where it serves the plan better.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bh5 improves the bishop's placement, targeting e8 and supporting the kingside attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: g4 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: g4 gains kingside space and supports a future attack on the enemy king.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Bg6 improves the bishop's placement. The bishop was less effective on h5 and moves to g6 where it serves the plan better.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bg6 improves the bishop's placement, shifting from h5 to a more effective diagonal.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Nh4 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on f3 and moves to h4 where it serves the plan better.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nh4 improves the knight from f3, aiming for g6 or f5 to pressure Black's kingside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Nxe4 captures the bishop. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nxe4 captures the bishop, changing the balance of material or pawn structure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Nxe4 captures the knight. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nxe4 captures the knight, changing the pawn structure or gaining material.

moveAnnotations.annotation: f3 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: f3 gains kingside space and supports a future attack toward the enemy king.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Bb3. This is the typical position arising from the Sadler Variation (3...Nf6 4.e3 Bg4). From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bb3 — typical Sadler Variation; key plans involve piece placement, pawn breaks, and targeting weaknesses.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Central Variation (3.e4) is an important variation of the Queen's Gambit Accepted. Against the aggressive 3.e4, Black plays ...e5 striking back in the center.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 3.e4 in the Queen's Gambit Accepted invites Black's ...e5 counterstrike in the center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds with d5, contesting the center. This is the move that defines the Central Variation (3.e4).
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's d5 contests the center, defining the Central Variation.

moveAnnotations.annotation: dxc4 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.

moveAnnotations.annotation: e4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e4 stakes a central claim, controlling space and restricting Black's piece activity.

moveAnnotations.annotation: e5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e5 stakes a central claim, controlling space and restricting White's piece activity.

moveAnnotations.annotation: exd4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: exd4 stakes a central pawn claim, controlling space and restricting Black's piece activity.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Ng5 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on f3 and moves to g5 where it serves the plan better.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Nxf7 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Nxh8 captures the rook. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Alekhine Variation (4.Nc3) is an important variation of the Queen's Gambit Accepted. White plays Nc3 before e3, going for the e4 push.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White plays 4.Nc3 in the Queen's Gambit Accepted, aiming for e4 to seize the center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds with d5, contesting the center. This is the move that defines the Alekhine Variation (4.Nc3).
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's d5 contests the center, defining the Alekhine Variation after 4.Nc3.

moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Qd5 develops the queen to a strong central post controlling key squares and supporting the center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Bb7 — Black fianchettoes the bishop, placing it on the long diagonal where it controls the center from a distance. This is a key positional idea.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bb7 fianchettoes the bishop, controlling the long diagonal and pressuring the center from a distance.
